title: Caramelised Onion Chutney

overview.description: A rich, sweet and savoury chutney made from slowly caramelised onions, vinegar and brown sugar.

ingredients: ½ tsp freshly ground black pepper
method: Heat the olive oil in a large heavy-based saucepan over medium heat.
Add the sliced onions and salt.
Cook for 10 minutes, stirring regularly, until the onions begin to soften.
Cook for 35–45 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the onions are deeply softened and golden brown.
Add the brown sugar, red wine vinegar, balsamic vinegar and golden syrup.
Add the black pepper and thyme.
Cook for 25–35 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the liquid has reduced and the chutney has become thick and glossy.
Remove the thyme stems if using whole sprigs.
Taste and adjust the balance of sweetness, acidity and seasoning.

learning.did-you-know: Caramelisation and the Maillard reaction both contribute to the deep flavour of cooked onions, although they are different chemical processes.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How slow cooking transforms onions into a sweet, deeply flavoured preserve and how vinegar balances sweetness in a chutney.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A versatile homemade chutney that can turn simple cheese, bread or leftovers into a much more interesting meal.
chapman-tips: Don't rush the onions; the slow cooking stage is where most of the flavour develops.
The chutney will thicken further as it cools.
Don't reduce it too far or it may become sticky rather than chutney-like.
The vinegar should remain noticeable enough to balance the sugar.
A mixture of red wine and balsamic vinegar gives more complexity than either alone.
The chutney is particularly good with strong cheddar.
Use a wide saucepan to help the liquid evaporate efficiently.
notes.batch-cooking: Freezes well in small portions. It can also be stored in the refrigerator for several weeks in a clean, sealed container.
notes.leftovers: Excellent with cheese sandwiches, burgers, sausages, pies and leftover roast meat.
